pattern: Handing out the smallest elements' shifts first, and always nudging each one just past whatever was claimed before it, packs the most distinct final values into the fewest wasted slots.
hints: Since every element can move independently within its own window of size 2k+1, think of this as greedily assigning each element the smallest still-available integer in its window, processed from the smallest original element to the largest.

Sort the array first. Keep track of the largest value assigned so far; for the next element, the best available new value is one more than that, unless the element's own window doesn't reach that low, in which case use the bottom of its window instead.

If even the smallest value in an element's window isn't larger than everything already assigned, that element simply can't contribute a new distinct value -- skip it and move on without changing what's already been claimed.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, every element becomes distinct
statement example, one duplicate cannot be separated
k is zero, nothing can move
value near the lower bound where the window could go negative
large uniform block whose window is too small to spread everyone out
already all distinct, k still helps nothing extra
huge k lets every element move but the array is short, so ties still resolve one at a time in sorted order

stress.description: random arrays with a small value range and moderate k so windows frequently overlap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = min(max(size, 1), 100000)
    k = rng.randint(0, 20)
    return [[rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)], k]
stress.note: a narrow value range with a small k forces many overlapping windows, the case the greedy must resolve correctly
